topic: A person's car is towed from their own driveway
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sound of the tow truck's air brakes squealing was the last thing I registered before the driver finally pulled away. I stood on the cracked concrete of my driveway, my hands still gripping the steering wheel, a faint tremor running through my arms. A slow, shaky exhale escaped my lungs, and I let my head drop back against the cool metal of the car door. It was over. The knot that had been tightening in my chest for the last hour finally loosened, replaced by a vast, empty space. I took a deep, shuddering breath of the afternoon air, and for the first time all day, my shoulders dropped an inch.

[story 2]

"Well, that's that," I muttered, watching the flashing lights recede down the street. I walked over to the vehicle, not to inspect the damage—I already knew the damage was done—but just to touch the familiar paintwork. My jaw unclenched, and a small, involuntary smile tugged at the corner of my mouth. The tension that had been coiled tight in my neck began to unwind, pooling in my limbs like warm water. I leaned my forehead against the cool glass, feeling the steady rhythm of my pulse finally settling back into a normal beat. Everything felt lighter, somehow.

[story 3]

When the tow operator finally nodded and drove off, I didn't move immediately. I just stood there, staring at the empty space where my sedan had been parked. A slow, deliberate blink cleared my vision. A wave of profound stillness washed over me. I started walking toward the house, my pace unhurried, almost buoyant. The tightness around my throat dissolved, and I could feel the warmth spreading through my chest cavity. I let out a soft, almost silent laugh—a sound I hadn't realized I'd been holding back—and finally, I allowed myself to simply stand still.
